summary: High‑energy set with scorching Dancin’ In The Street and tight jams
blurb: The Boston Tea Party show bursts with fiery renditions of Mason’s Children, Hard To Handle and a marathon Dancin’ In The Street. Jerry’s guitar is deep, hard‑pushing and “dirty‑nasty,” while Tom Constanten adds thick keyboard runs. Recording quality is good overall despite a bit of muddiness and hiss.
review:
On December 29 1969 the Dead turned the Boston Tea Party into a New Year’s Eve party that still feels fresh today. The first set rolls from the folk‑rock opener Cold Rain And Snow through bluesy Black Peter and Easy Wind, then kicks up the voltage with Hard To Handle and Mason’s Children. By the second set the band is in full swing – Cumberland Blues and Casey Jones give way to a 15‑minute, scorching Dancin’ In The Street that showcases Garcia’s “deep, hard‑pushing, dirty‑nasty” guitar work, while Tom Constanten’s thick keyboard runs swirl through the mix. Even with a little room‑ambience hiss, the soundboard captures the band’s tight banter and the crowd‑sing‑along vibe on Dire Wolf, making this one of the more memorable late‑’60s performances.

Both available recordings are soundboard sources that rate “good.” The version identified as gd69-12-29.sbd.8044.sbeok.shnf is the clearest, offering a balanced mix with only minor muddiness. Fans looking for the definitive listening experience should start there – it delivers the energy and nuance of a night when the Dead were still honing the improvisational fire that would define the ’70s.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Both recordings are marked positive, repeatedly praising standout songs (Mason’s Children, Hard To Handle, Dancin’ In The Street) and highlighting strong performances (Garcia’s guitar, Tom Constanten's keyboards). No consistent negative comments about the band's playing appear; only a single note of a poor rendition of Good Lovin' does not outweigh the overall enthusiasm.

best_recording.reason: Good soundboard capture with balanced mix; only slight muddiness and hiss, making it the clearest representation of the performance.

key_highlights: Scorching 15‑minute Dancin’ In The Street that drives the second set.
Garcia’s deep, hard‑pushing guitar tone on multiple numbers.
Tom Constanten’s thick keyboard runs add texture throughout.
Overall good soundboard quality despite minor muddiness.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers deep, hard‑pushing, ‘dirty‑nasty’ guitar especially on Dancin’ In The Street and urges crowd sing‑along during Dire Wolf.
band_performance.Mickey: Mickey’s drumming sits back, less prominent in this era as the rhythm section begins to shift.
band_performance.Keys: Tom Constanten’s thick keyboard runs are highlighted throughout, adding a rich layer to the jams.
